title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Career
meta_description: Learn to trust your gut with career decisions. This article shows how intuition can guide you when logic alone falls short.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Career
og_description: Learn to trust your gut with career decisions. This article shows how intuition can guide you when logic alone falls short.
body:
The Career Decision That Almost Broke Me

I remember sitting in my car in the parking lot of a company I'd worked at for six years. My hands were shaking. I had an offer from another firm - more money, better title, the whole package. Everything on paper said "take it." But my gut was screaming something else. Not in words. More like a dull, heavy feeling in my chest that wouldn't go away.

I took the job anyway. Because that's what smart people do, right? They follow the logic, the numbers, the clear path forward. Three months later, I was miserable. The culture was toxic. My boss was a nightmare. And I'd ignored the one thing that had been trying to warn me all along: my intuition.

That was ten years ago. Since then, I've learned something that changed everything about how I make career decisions. And I want to share it with you, because chances are, you've been in that same parking lot - maybe not literally, but emotionally. You've felt that pull between what looks good on paper and what feels right in your body.

Let me be clear about something from the start: I'm not saying you should ignore logic or throw spreadsheets out the window. What I'm saying is that your intuition is a legitimate, reliable source of information that most of us have been trained to dismiss. And when it comes to career decisions - the kind that shape your life, your energy, your relationships - ignoring that inner signal is a recipe for regret.

Why Your Intuition Gets Drowned Out at Work

Here's the thing about modern work culture: it's built on a model that treats intuition like a liability. From the time you enter school, you're rewarded for logic, analysis, and quantifiable results. "Show your work," they said. "Prove it." And somewhere along the way, you learned that feelings don't count as proof.

But your nervous system didn't get that memo. It's still processing information at a speed your conscious mind can't match. Your intuition is picking up on subtle cues - the way a potential boss's smile doesn't reach their eyes, the tension in a team meeting that everyone pretends isn't there, the quiet exhaustion that follows certain conversations. These aren't random feelings. They're data points your body is collecting in real time.

Does that land? Because I want you to really sit with it. Your intuition isn't some mystical woo-woo thing reserved for psychics and fortune tellers. It's a survival mechanism that's been fine-tuned over thousands of years. And it's still working, even if you've learned to ignore it.

The Three Types of Intuitive Signals in Your Body

Before we get into the practical steps, you need to know what you're looking for. Intuition doesn't always come as a clear voice or a sudden knowing. More often, it shows up as physical sensations that we've learned to rationalize away.

1. The Gut Feeling - This is the classic one, and it's real. You know that sinking sensation in your stomach when something's off? Or the lightness you feel when something's right? That's your enteric nervous system - sometimes called your "second brain" - sending signals. It's not random. It's responding to environmental cues your conscious mind hasn't processed yet.

2. The Chest Tightness or Expansion - Your heart area is another major intuitive center. When a decision aligns with your deeper truth, you might feel a sense of openness or warmth in your chest. When it doesn't, you'll feel constriction, heaviness, or that "weight on your chest" sensation. Don't dismiss this as anxiety. Sometimes anxiety is just intuition trying to get your attention through the only channel that works.

3. The Full-Body Signal - This is the most reliable one, in my experience. When you think about a decision, does your whole body feel like it's leaning forward or pulling back? Do you feel energized or drained? Your body knows before your mind does. It's just a matter of learning to listen.

If you're new to this, try this simple exercise right now: Think about your current job. Notice what happens in your body. Don't judge it. Just observe. Then think about a career change you've been considering. Notice the difference. That's your intuition speaking.

Why Most Career Advice Fails You

Let's be honest about something. The standard career advice out there - "follow your passion," "just take the leap," "do what you love and the money will follow" - it's not just unhelpful. It's actually damaging. Because it puts all the pressure on you to have perfect clarity before you move. And that's not how intuition works.

Intuition doesn't give you the whole picture. It gives you the next step. That's it. One step at a time. So when you're sitting there thinking, "I need to know exactly what career change to make before I do anything," you're setting yourself up for paralysis. Your intuition is more like a flashlight in a dark room than a floodlight. It shows you just enough to take the next step.

"Your intuition doesn't need to know the whole path. It only needs to show you the next step. Trust that step, then wait for the next one to light up. This is how you walk through the dark without falling."

The Practical approach: How to Actually Use Intuition for Career Decisions

Okay, let's get into the actual process. This is a four-step method I've developed over years of working with people who've been told they're "too sensitive" or "overthink everything." You're not overthinking. You're just not using the right filter.

Step 1: Separate Fear from Intuition

This is the hardest part, and it's where most people get stuck. Fear and intuition can feel identical in your body. Both create tightness, both speed up your heart, both make you want to pull back. So how do you tell them apart?

Here's the key difference: Fear is about survival. It's trying to keep you safe from a perceived threat. Intuition is about alignment. It's trying to guide you toward what's true for you, even if that's uncomfortable.

The way to distinguish them is to ask yourself one question: "Does this feeling contract or expand?" Fear contracts. It makes you smaller, tighter, more defensive. Intuition, even when it's pointing you toward something hard, creates a sense of expansion. You might still feel nervous, but there's a thread of "yes" running through it.

Let me give you an example. Say you're considering leaving a stable job to start your own business. Fear says: "You'll fail. You'll run out of money. Everyone will judge you." That's contraction. Intuition says: "This is scary, but I can feel the pull. There's something here I need to explore." That's expansion, even in the fear.

If you're still unsure, try this: Write down the decision you're facing. Then write down everything you're afraid of. Then write down what you feel when you imagine the best-case scenario. Your intuition lives in that second list.

Step 2: Create Space for the Signal

You can't hear your intuition when you're in constant motion. I know you're busy. I know you have deadlines, meetings, and a to-do list that never ends. But if you want to make better career decisions, you have to build in moments of stillness.

This doesn't mean you need to meditate for an hour every morning (though if that works for you, great). It means you need to create small pockets of quiet where you can check in with your body. Here are a few ways to do that:

  • Take a 5-minute walk without your phone. Let your mind wander. Notice what comes up.
  • Before you make any decision, take three deep breaths. Then ask yourself: "What do I really feel about this?"
  • Keep a simple journal by your bed. Before you sleep, write down one career-related question and the first answer that comes to mind without editing it.
  • Try a body scan when you're feeling stuck. Close your eyes and mentally scan from your head to your toes. Where do you feel tension? Where do you feel ease? That's your intuition mapping itself out.

One of the best tools I've found for this kind of work is a guided journal that helps you ask the right questions. See on Amazon (paid link) - it's designed specifically for people who want to strengthen their intuitive connection without feeling like they're forcing it.

Step 3: Test the Signal with Small Actions

Here's where most people get tripped up. They think intuition means you have to make a huge, dramatic leap. It doesn't. Intuition is best used in small, low-stakes experiments that give you more information.

Let's say you're considering a career change into a creative field. Your intuition says "yes," but your fear is screaming "no." Instead of quitting your job tomorrow, test it. Take one small action that moves you in that direction. Sign up for a weekend workshop. Have coffee with someone who does that work. Spend 30 minutes a day on a side project.

Pay attention to how each small action feels. Does it energize you or drain you? Does it open up more possibilities or close them down? That feedback loop is your intuition giving you real-time data. You don't have to know the whole path. You just have to take the next right step and see what happens.

This approach works because it bypasses the part of your brain that needs certainty. You're not committing to anything big. You're just gathering information through experience. And experience is the language your intuition speaks most fluently.

Step 4: Learn to Recognize Your Personal Intuitive Style

Not everyone experiences intuition the same way. Some people get clear images or words. Others get physical sensations. Some get sudden "knowings" that seem to come out of nowhere. And some people just get a vague sense of "yes" or "no" without any detail.

None of these is better than the others. The key is to learn what your style is so you can trust it. Here's a quick way to figure that out: Think back to a time when you made a decision that turned out to be right, even though you couldn't explain why at the time. How did that knowing come to you? Was it a feeling? A thought? A dream? A coincidence that felt meaningful?

That's your signature. Start paying attention to how it shows up in smaller decisions - what to eat, who to call, which route to take. The more you practice with low-stakes choices, the more you'll recognize the signal when it matters.

For people who are highly sensitive or empathic, this process can be overwhelming because you pick up on everyone else's energy too. If that sounds like you, I recommend reading See on Amazon (paid link) - it's one of the most grounded books I've found for learning how to filter out noise and hear your own inner voice clearly.

What to Do When Your Intuition Says "No" to a Good Opportunity

This is the situation that trips up even experienced intuitive people. You've got a job offer that looks perfect on paper. The money is good. The title is a step up. Your friends and family are excited for you. But something in your gut is saying "no."

First, don't ignore it. I've seen too many people take a "good on paper" job and end up miserable within six months. Your intuition is picking up on something you can't see yet - maybe the company culture, maybe the boss's true nature, maybe the way this role will drain your energy in ways you can't predict.

Second, don't act on the "no" immediately. Instead, get curious. Ask yourself: "What is this 'no' trying to protect me from? What is it pointing me toward?" Sometimes the "no" is a full stop. Sometimes it's a "not yet" or a "not this way."

Third, create a condition that would make the "no" turn into a "yes." What would need to change? If you can't think of anything, then the "no" is probably a full stop. If you can think of something, you've just turned a vague feeling into actionable information.

I had a client once who turned down a six-figure job because her intuition said "no." Everyone thought she was crazy. Six months later, the company went through a massive layoff and the culture turned toxic. She landed a role that paid less but gave her way more freedom and fulfillment. Her intuition had seen the writing on the wall before anyone else could.

How to Handle the Doubt That Follows Every Intuitive Decision

Let's be real: even after you make a decision based on intuition, the doubt will come. It always does. Your mind will start running scenarios: "What if I made the wrong choice? What if I'm just being lazy? What if I'm missing out?"

This is normal. It doesn't mean you made the wrong decision. It means your mind is trying to protect you from uncertainty. The trick is to not let that doubt erase the knowing you had before you acted.

One way to handle this is to keep a record of your intuitive hits. Write down the decisions you made based on feeling, and then write down what happened as a result. Over time, you'll build evidence that your intuition is reliable. That evidence makes it easier to trust the next time.

Another strategy is to give yourself a "decision deadline." Intuition works best when you're not forcing it. If you're stuck in a loop of doubt, set a timer for 24 hours. Tell yourself you're allowed to worry for exactly one day, and then you're going to commit to your decision and move forward. This gives your mind time to process without letting it run the show forever.

If you want a deeper dive into how to work with doubt and build trust in your inner guidance, I highly recommend See on Amazon (paid link) - it's a practical, no-nonsense guide that doesn't ask you to believe anything you don't already know in your bones.

When Your Career Intuition Is Pointing You Toward a Major Change

Sometimes the intuitive signal isn't subtle. It's a persistent pull that won't go away, no matter how much you try to rationalize it away. You might be in a job that's fine - good pay, decent people, reasonable hours - but there's this quiet voice that keeps saying "there's more."

That voice is worth listening to. Not because you need to quit tomorrow, but because it's pointing you toward growth. The danger isn't in following it too quickly. The danger is in ignoring it for so long that you wake up ten years from now wondering what happened to your life.

Here's a practical way to approach this: Give yourself permission to explore without pressure. Spend 20 minutes a week imagining what your ideal career would look like. Don't worry about how to make it happen. Just let your imagination run. Notice what comes up. That's your intuition showing you the direction.

Then, take one small action each week that moves you in that direction. It could be as simple as updating your LinkedIn profile, saving one article about that field, or talking to one person who does that work. Small actions build momentum. And momentum is what turns an intuitive pull into a real change.

The Bottom Line

Your intuition is not a luxury. It's not a nice-to-have. It's a survival tool that's been trying to guide you your whole life. The problem isn't that you don't have it. The problem is that you've been taught to ignore it.

Every time you make a career decision that goes against your gut, you're telling yourself that your inner knowing doesn't matter. And that erodes trust in yourself over time. But every time you honor that signal - even when it's inconvenient, even when it doesn't make sense to anyone else - you're rebuilding that trust. You're proving to yourself that you can be relied upon.

Start small. The next time you're faced with a decision at work, pause. Take a breath. Check in with your body. See what it's telling you. You don't have to act on it right away. Just notice it. That's the first step toward making decisions that don't just look good on paper, but feel good in your life.

And if you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've put together a free assessment that helps you identify where you're currently blocking your intuition and what to do about it. Take the quiz here - it takes about five minutes and will give you a clear starting point for your next career move.
